techniques.name: The MICRO postion of the kiura control to the the cross body armbar postion
techniques.concept: The Kiumra lock out postion is use to create space and allow for better control durng a transiton.

stepsList.text: From Kimura control, hand on the wrist will "throttle" the opps wrist
the hand on top of your wrist is going to point up as you EXTEND your arm out. this is lock out pressure
as you apply lock out pressure LEAN towards the head, the leg behind the op will kick stand out and the leg on the head straightens out pressure down
establish cross body arm bar position. lock out still hips engaged
lock out the armbar using th methods gone

techniques.name: Bullfighter Pass with Shoulder Drop
techniques.concept: Blading your body to on sid to drop into postiom
techniques.steps:
Establish bullfighter grips, push INTO legs
Break hooks on forarms by squeezing arms and straightening them out
Pull legss down to one side and start passing on the outside
Ass opp establishes frames on shoulders when you pass, BLADE your body to drop into position
get danger leg in to Hip line quickly as possible
Exchange head side pant grip to lapel grip.( The remaining hand can weave between legs or grab pant leg, Peters edit idk if good )
establish side control

stepsList.text: Establish bullfighter grips, push INTO legs
Break hooks on forarms by squeezing arms and straightening them out
Pull legss down to one side and start passing on the outside
Ass opp establishes frames on shoulders when you pass, BLADE your body to drop into position
get danger leg in to Hip line quickly as possible
Exchange head side pant grip to lapel grip.( The remaining hand can weave between legs or grab pant leg, Peters edit idk if good )
establish side control

techniques.name: Bullfighter Pass with Shoulder Drop with Step Over Pass
techniques.concept: Works because Opp is expecting you to keep figting for same side pin, but you go over the lgs to come from the other side.
techniques.steps:
Follow steps in the Bullfighter Pass with Shoulder Drop. However this time you face lost of resistance in trying to pass
The Far/Leg arm presses down on the opps top leg to clear an opening
step over the legs with the BOTTOM leg first
As you clear the legs begin to LOOK up to the direction you are going.
The arm on the inside of the opp hip looks for lapel or shoulder control while the other arm looks for back of collar grip.
establish side pin

stepsList.text: Follow steps in the Bullfighter Pass with Shoulder Drop. However this time you face lost of resistance in trying to pass
The Far/Leg arm presses down on the opps top leg to clear an opening
step over the legs with the BOTTOM leg first
As you clear the legs begin to LOOK up to the direction you are going.
The arm on the inside of the opp hip looks for lapel or shoulder control while the other arm looks for back of collar grip.
establish side pin

techniques.name: Bullfighter Pass with Shoulder Drop with Step Over Head ( Open all the way
techniques.concept: This works beacues this time you pass all they way around the head, you not looking for a side pin from where you dropped in
techniques.steps:
Complete Bullfighter Pass with Shoulder Drop
this time instead of loooking for a knee at the hip line you continue to walk around up to the opponents head
the leg that is close to the opps head will step over the head and place knee behind
use your bottom elbow to frame where the hip is at as you move to the other side.
the leg that is still in the air can step around as your elbow frame hand looks for lapel
get side pin

stepsList.text: Complete Bullfighter Pass with Shoulder Drop
this time instead of loooking for a knee at the hip line you continue to walk around up to the opponents head
the leg that is close to the opps head will step over the head and place knee behind

keyInsights.text: Drive into the opp when passing to stay heavy

techniques.name: Elbow Escape from Mount — Variation 5
techniques.concept: Make the KNEE the focus — everything is about creating access to the knee when opponent is tight.
techniques.steps:
Details: Primary frame = arm across hip.
Secondary frame = hand covering primary arm, elbow on opponent's knee.
Keep elbow close to opponent's hip throughout the shrimp — this preserves both frames.

Steps:
1. Establish primary frame (arm across hip) + secondary frame (elbow on knee).
2. Execute rising shrimp, keeping elbow pinned to opponent's hip.
3. Roll onto shoulder. Straighten same-side leg and thread it between opponent's legs.
4. Recover guard.

Steps:
1. Establish primary frame (arm across hip) + secondary frame (elbow on knee).
2. Execute rising shrimp, keeping elbow pinned to opponent's hip.
3. Roll onto shoulder. Straighten same-side leg and thread it between opponent's legs.
4. Recover guard.

⚠ Never let elbow drift toward your belly button — this collapses the frame entirely.

entries.notes: Imported from Instructional note: Elbow Escape from Mount — Variation 5

techniques.name: Half Guard Pass
techniques.concept: Need to sit back far enough that my shoulder line is under the knee line in opponents half guard
techniques.steps:
Establish over under grip on pant leg,thru knee shield,  and grab opponent lapel around collar bone level. Keep elbow tucked
Slide knee down to opponents heel in the half guard. Shoulders should now be below knee line.
punch pant leg down, pull lapel grip and Tripod up and free knee to escape from half guard. you may need to get creative with knee movement to free knee . You can use lapel grip to steer opponents head for stronger pass
clear knee line and get near sit hip Knee into J-point.

stepsList.text: Establish over under grip on pant leg,thru knee shield,  and grab opponent lapel around collar bone level. Keep elbow tucked
Slide knee down to opponents heel in the half guard. Shoulders should now be below knee line.

punch pant leg down, pull lapel grip and Tripod up and free knee to escape from half guard. you may need to get creative with knee movement to free knee . You can use lapel grip to steer opponents head for stronger pass
clear knee line and get near sit hip Knee into J-point.

techniques.name: Side Pin to Back Takel Gift Wrap with submissions
techniques.concept: Your opponent is gonna try to go belly down for an escape but you need to react faster
techniques.steps:
from the side pin, release knee grip, take a big step around opponents back landing somewhere near shoulder blades
at the same time take top arm and place elbow at J point and throw the other leg behind you. its one big spin move
establish gift wrap and control

stepsList.text: from the side pin, release knee grip, take a big step around opponents back landing somewhere near shoulder blades
at the same time take top arm and place elbow at J point and throw the other leg behind you. its one big spin move
establish gift wrap and control

keyInsights.text: When passing from half guard with knee shield, get below the knee line
keep knee high on the head, keep elbow holstered and use the figure four to control movement.

techniques.name: Bow and Arrrow Choke w
techniques.concept: Lean/fall TOWARDS the leg that is under hooked. DO NOT sit back like a conventional bow and arrow
techniques.steps:
Establish a good side control with a gift wrap. Can grab write or go writ over wrist.
Secure belt line hook
From figure 4 , use tod punch down and away to trap, and use other hand to secure collar grip. Use 2 fingers and index thumb. Can use other hand to pull slack out of collar
Once secure. LEAN/FALL towards the legs, then under hook top leg.
Try to lock your guard or at cover the shoulder  that’s of the ground
Compress legs while pulling the collar to sink choke

stepsList.text: Establish a good side control with a gift wrap. Can grab write or go writ over wrist.
Secure belt line hook

techniques.name: Armband from Bow and arrow choke
techniques.steps:
From the bow and arrow choke,  look for arm and hook it
Maintain undercooked leg and arm and apply and arm bar

stepsList.text: From the bow and arrow choke,  look for arm and hook it
Maintain undercooked leg and arm and apply and arm bar

debriefs.fix: need to react to a side pin or take down more quickly. dont let them settle in

stepsList.text: From Kimura control, hand on the wrist will "throttle" the opps wrist
stepsList.concept: this further breaks the chain of control on opps wrist.

stepsList.text: the hand on top of your wrist is going to point up as you EXTEND your arm out. this is lock out pressure
stepsList.concept: this creates alot of pressure on the opps arm that its hard for them to do

instructionalNotes.name: Overhook Entry
instructionalNotes.concept: Platform and forward drive created makes this work.

stepsList.text: Established side control, legs split out. One under hook on far arm, control the near arm
Drive near arm hand across to under hook side connect hands then transition to gable grip
stepsList.concept: KEEP SHOULDER LOW UNDER OPPS WRIST

stepsList.text: Use gable grip to pull arm into you and drive the same side leg thru to establish your armbar platform
stepsList.concept: Slow and deliberate to minimize energy l

stepsList.text: As you pull arm into you, the wrist will fall into your hands. LOCK your kimura control position .

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.name: Responding to the unerhook escape: no underhook in.
instructionalNotes.concept: YOu need to be aware of the underhook escape and beat it
instructionalNotes.details: Dave makes an interesting point because he is a small guy he refer to this "BUNGIE" Concept. Where he applies pressure or allows space depending on the situation. He allows space to move when he feels unstable and applies pressure when stable.

stepsList.text: You are in a side control with No underhooks. Opponent goes for a bump and tries to shoot an

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.name: One frame and one arm foing for underhook
instructionalNotes.concept: Opp wants to shoot the underhook and get to a single leg takedown. DONT let it happen.
instructionalNotes.details:
At times the bottom arm cab become a problem if you dont address it. Lets say you have the under hook already and the opponent gets the bottom arm out and is able to move it around. 

A just grab behind the ELbow Pull it towars you and then step over head 

or 
get to Staple the other arm down witha knee and get to the Kimura control.

Here dave pulls the opp up to the side, (does not sprawl) cuz the arm is pinned by the knee.
instructionalNotes.warning: Avoid the single leg take down from here.. track his movements.

stepsList.text: Opp has arm in place for under hook escape. Turn and FACE the elbow/arm. this kills that underhook
stepsList.concept: You get into that offset side control position.

stepsList.text: the hand under the under hook arm rest under the elbow ( make a fist the the thumb press on the outside)
Let the op go for the under hook, Your far leg straightens out and post to mange distance. leg side head still post out for balance
Top arm press of the ground here while the bladed hand shoots into the space create by the escape attempt
stepsList.concept: Drop elbow in the rib chest area and CHOKE that arm. Head has to go DOWN

stepsList.text: Keep an eye on the legs! grab the bottom leg and pull over your head to secure the position ( op can not escape). Can also pull the other leg for a different control.
transition to the kimura control position.
stepsList.concept:
OPEN YOUR BASE UP AND PUT YOUR WEIGHT FORWARD. DONT OVER KNEES MAKES YOU EASY TO MOVE LIKE A TOWER. .

Can usr ops gi to secure the arm.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.chapterTitle: Ch 1: Side Control - Underhook Entry To Kimura Control (0:00)
instructionalNotes.name: Side Control with under hook
instructionalNotes.details:
Key grip when Dave establish his side control, he does not stop at gable grip he goes for forearm deep grip. 

Stay in tune with ops movement keep one knee at hip and the other leg ( head side straighten out)

If needed use your knee ( hip switch to clear the arm to keep it on the outside (elbow away from body) or smash it/ pin it to.

stepsList.text: WIth underhook grip pull it in /slide close to your body and put pressure on ribs / sternum. get opps back of ground by SPRAWL
stepsList.concept: important to sprawl so you maintain pressure here. do not pull. " DOnt let the tiger out the cage". can move weight back and forth when op is defending.

stepsList.text: free arm look to post behind opps head and  push hand to ward your knee point fingers AWAY from you.
stepsList.concept: at this point the leg on head side should be posted out and close to your opps head.

stepsList.text: Bend elbow to push opps head a little farther from you and step over
stepsList.concept: this  little bend will clear the head.

stepsList.text: as you clear the head keep your weight driving forward.chest plate cover the shoulder.
stepsList.concept: do not sit up, this is where we keep op control. drive forward.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.chapterTitle: Ch 1: Side Control - Underhook Entry To Kimura Control (0:00)
instructionalNotes.name: Kimura control from Knee on belly
instructionalNotes.concept: Give your opp the space wnated to get a knee on belly.
instructionalNotes.details: Its okay to put your hands on the mat sometimes, its a lot of stability provided.

stepsList.text: When opp frames on upper body, post on mat and get to knee on belly
Pummel your arm inside to get to the under hook
stepsList.concept: Look away to get the right angle and get tight

stepsList.text: as you pummel scoop the near side elbow to open it up and clear the space there

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.chapterTitle: Ch 1: Side Control - Underhook Entry To Kimura Control (0:00)
instructionalNotes.name: Underhook to Kimura Control using C-grip on wrist and driving head thru.
instructionalNotes.concept: Beat the frames and hip movement you opponent will present. Use a C
instructionalNotes.details: In a classic side control position your opp will have arms to chest ( t-rex) and dave was on his HIPS ( 1 leg forward and 1 leg back) for side control. He said we must track the hips and address the frames. He Appllies pressure and creates space to be mobile.  Kazushi back and forth : example opp frames on your chest you sit BACK into hips. iff ops tries to hip escape by bringing elbows down, you drive Forward, and pull on the elbow.

stepsList.text: Op has trex arms, in your side control under hook side cuffs the shoulder
stepsList.concept: really pull that shoulder in to hold him at bay

stepsList.text: c grip on top wrist, press forward and drive head into the space. pull arm over use your shoulder
stepsList.concept: use your head to create leverage

stepsList.text: switch your base/ hips and Drop your elbow over your opps hear to establish position. USE ELBOW TO DISRUPT SPINE ALIGNMENT.
stepsList.concept: Knee close to the hips will track that hip and the knee by head will be straightens out provide base.,

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.name: Troubleshooting crossbody postion.
instructionalNotes.goal: Understanding the Crossbody armbar postion.
instructionalNotes.details:
The cross body arm bar position changes a bout depending on your grip. 
Traditio kimura grip from a side pin postion lets you enter the crossboty with opening to attack head side and from gross body. 

Entering with reverse kimura lets opens Leg and cross body situations

If an opponent tryin to stack you from the cross body position, don't just stay sitting up , Put back into ground and extend back. control from there, cross legs and dig into position. 

Leg over head escape is the main issue with cross body. - be ahead of curve keep an eye on the leg that can get thrown over. 

1. if he opp gets leg over, split legs. You can go to a triangle or attack the other arm. 


2. Elbow drop escape from cross body- you can shift your weight around to spin  around to thee side and attack other arm or keep spinning to end up in a mount position and start again. Or let the elbow drop happen, throw legg over and let op get to knees then go for a back take.

cross body system works from closed guard too

3. if op traps you r foot in crossbody postion, leg assist and commiti to armbar.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.chapterTitle: Ch 3: Leg Side - The Bladed Fulcrum (21:52)
instructionalNotes.name: Leg side - Armbar
instructionalNotes.goal: Achive an armbar on the leg side with RNC defense
instructionalNotes.details:
Leg side armbar 
Have a folcurm in place, blade of arm in the pockert of hip, legs point into your ops direction pining heels, lay to the leg side and hug leg. 
As you fall dont PULL lean toward where the fulcrum is pointing. ( keeps forces in tact)

Dave like to keep his head up a bit so that he can kick it back down, at the same time he drives the fulcrum arm UP. 
as the grip breaks he slides the fulcrum arm up to the wrist and he gets an "arrow head" (wrist wrip and bicpet hug grip)grip for the finish.

TROUBLE SHOOOTING. 
If as i fall to break the grip and go for a armbar, he pushes his head through my legs to try to free the elbow. SIT UP and trap opp ( one leg under his head and the other over the chest position)to regain control. if i stay down he will free the elbow. 
Can also put leg on body inside the head and turn towards the leg side for another armbar finish. 

OR you can see that the arm you are trying to get is gettin g to dangerous ( starts putting hand on the pants) you can grab that hands cuff  and swivle to the other side for an armbar on the other side. you kind of end up in a S mount position and can attack an armbar -HIP ALL THE WAY TO SHOULDER 

go into what the previous lesson taught, chnage grip face fulcrum away know and split legs ( he says flare) to keep op in place.

then go back to the cross body finish. 

If opponent goes to Phase 2 defense ( gable grip ) ,you can do what we did in last lesson and pin wrist to chest with feet and triangle (leg assit)

Trouble shooting 2. 



lets say in the leg side armbar Op throws your leg to his legs, you want to leg assist over your ops hip and continue attack . 


Troubleshoot 3. 
lets say op is able to split legs and sit up, leg on body comes to near side of ops head. bottom pleg posts on ops neck, go for armbar
instructionalNotes.concept: Lay on the ground commit to the positon.
instructionalNotes.warning: Leg split pin. be cautious when the op is resisitng cuz he can trhow the body leg towards his legs to trap, but you cna go for a back take there.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.name: Knee on Belly Armbar
instructionalNotes.goal: Armbar from Knee on belly
instructionalNotes.details:
Armbar from knee on belly - one knee under armpit and the other over head. ( not as strong as cross body armbar)
Dave uses a judo grip. Use knee on belly concepts grap the far side lapel ( push to pin/exert downward pressure.
shoot shin into armpit
Other arm grips at floating elbow and pulls up to sit into a arm-bar position. 
Judo grip on the lapel the is used to pull opp into you. trap opp to you.
if op slips elbow you can recover knee on belly.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.chapterTitle: Ch 1: Head Side - Lion Killer Finish (0:00)
instructionalNotes.name: Head side - The RNC grip on the Armbar from side pin
instructionalNotes.goal: Using a fulcrum system to Pry open an opponents RNC armbar defense on head side
instructionalNotes.details:
Phase one- RNC defense to break 
Have good control 
You will sway in this position
Lock in on rnc grip on the arm. Elbow over hip and blade tucked into hip. 
lean to Head side 
drive hips forward
counter act opps movement with legs ( split them open) and counter hitch hiker by pulling elbow and straighten/rolling out. 

Disrupt the spine with heel in the spine with heel to neck 

Phase two - RNC to gable grip defense to break 
same set-up lean to head side
place top leg over the wrists and pin to body ( semi triangle) - this is called a leg assist
really pin the wrist together and eliminate the space between wrist and chest. 
thrust hips to break grip and lock in grip then rolling up. Here you will start of in RNC grip then you transfer to the two on one grip. keep legs pin to keep opponent down 


TROUBLESHOOTING
beat phase1 then you off to phase 2 and throws top leg over and splits your legs.
1. as the head comes out leg on ground strighten out and the lg on the body kick back into opps body. MAINTAIN control here. You dont want opp to get past your KNEELINE so that the back is to the ground. 
2. Bring opps elbow to body (this opens op a little more then bring the leg on the body thru the grips and lock in a triangle. 
3, top keg kinda pins opps chest to keep down.
4.
instructionalNotes.warning:
Dont change grips if it doesn't makes sense ( double on the wrist, RNC)
Stay cool in all postions, even if you do get a grip break or a counter- response

carful with sitting up when trying to finish you trade pressure for a new position.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.name: Armbar from Mount - Ralph Gracie Special
instructionalNotes.concept: Disrupt the spine to get to the S mount
instructionalNotes.warning:
Stay off line. 
Use hands on mat.
Keep cautious of the legs that is laying on the ground, tuck it so the opponent is not able to get the half guard.

stepsList.text: Get to mount and secure a cross collar grip.
Really turn your body.Lower chest to behind the defending elbow.
place hand on mat behind the elbow and drive using the same side leg to DRIVE the arm from one side to the other
once on thier other side DROP your elbow and PULL elbow back (disrupt the spine)
slide knee up to S mount then continue to armbar.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.name: Armbar from Mount
instructionalNotes.concept: Stay off line with the oppeonent when in mount, have one leg as a grape vine and the other ready to get to Knee on Belly (running leg)
instructionalNotes.warning:
Stay off line as far as possible so the opponent has to drive father careful with staying IN line.
carf

stepsList.text: Get to mount. Have 1 grapevine and one running leg. Stay off line
Initiate response by adding a cross face to the side you are leaning towards. Right on the jaw line. DISRUPT THE SPINE
As the opp grabs the cross face hand, place free hand on the mat and SHOOT the same side knee HIGH up so that the elbow is on your thigh.
Cuz you are posted bring running leg across into S mount.
Situate position. Stay heavy on op, have kick stand by opp head
you will drive the inside arm first then switvh witht he outside to secure a deep hold, FOLCRUM your arm into hip placement and then lean into the legs
can bring the kick stand leg over op head to finish armbar

instructionalNotes.seriesName: The Armlock Obsession — The Armlock Obsession
instructionalNotes.chapterTitle: Ch 4: Mid Attack - Lion Killer Finish (25:42)
instructionalNotes.name: Attacking a tradtional armbar from the MID postion.
instructionalNotes.concept: Breaking an armbar RNC defence using a RNC frip on arm.
instructionalNotes.warning: Be aware of the arm length short arms means less space to work with and long arms gives them more confidence to move.

stepsList.text: Establish a RNC grip on the arm and have close guard on armpit. you want to SAW/RATCHET side to side (dynamic). Disrupt a straight line of power by going side to side
Emotional side - you need to SHOW your passion when attacking this MID Position.
if needed lean towards the leg side, can stuff into armpit for stronger break.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.chapterTitle: Ch 4: Holding And Controlling A Closed Guard (31:16)
instructionalNotes.name: Holding the closed guard by attacking posture and balence
instructionalNotes.goal: Holding the closed guard by attacking posture and balence
instructionalNotes.details:
---Treat Ops back as a lever, we get better control at the top of lever then down by hips------

Dealing with the elbow in thighs tactic - get behind op elbows and pull op forward and do knee pull. then hug to keep postire down 

dealing with lapel frame and belt hold. Get figure 4 grip on cuff and as op postures pull IN with the legs and break grip . Get ELBOW across mid line ( Vul.1 ) or get hand on floor (Vul.2).

NOw opp may defend the grip break by bending arm down, reach over the back and pull into you end in a side scissor guard. 

Another situation , get cross collar grip and knee pull to break poster of opp


Hands and hips drill - goal get opps ass or hands on mat 

get op in closed guard and practice the timing. have opp stand up then knee pull before 2nd step. if opp posture all the way up take opp back ( hip bump sweep)

Use KNEE PULLs

dont pull straight into hip you want to pull UP bring knees up to armpits) and down to your guard. its easier to do that vs a pull straight down.  break posture. 
when opp head is over hips you can hit this 


As Opp goes to stand. 
on the before 2nd foot comes to stand Hit a knee pull to break posture. has to be before 2nd foot.

Even if Opp gets to 2 feet go for a knee pull to create force pulling down 

if opp is on knees and is in a vertical posture, can go for a hip bump sweep 

Head over hips pull forward. Head over hips push over. 


You can create more pressure in the close guard by driving feet/push hips into opps and then pull ( like a jack knife). can create alot of off balence. 

strenghten knee pulls- if opp stands fram on ankles and knee pull back down.
instructionalNotes.concept: Keep opps on Knees and posture in 45 degress and KNEEEEEE PULL (knee up to armpits)

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.chapterTitle: Ch 3: A Key Insight: Who Is Really On Top? (26:37)
instructionalNotes.name: Who is really on top in closed guard?
instructionalNotes.details:
As our hips and legs are on top off ops hips in closed guard, I am really in a top position. 

Powerful lower half is in a better position. 

My hips and legs are stronger than the ops upper body.
instructionalNotes.concept: Closed guard is really a top position

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.chapterTitle: Ch 2: General Overview Of The Closed Guard (7:36)
instructionalNotes.name: Getting into close guard best postions
instructionalNotes.goal: Create Kuzushi p in 1 of th 4 scenarios and exploit the 6 vulnerabilites.
instructionalNotes.details:
1. Ops elbow line crosses mid section > leads to side scissor. ( back exposure )
2. Bring Ops hands to the the floor > Leads to the Clamp Guard ( Submissions )
3. Inside wrist grip ( not a C grip but a U grip, stack wrist over forearm). can use this to dominate insde position and lead to submissions.

4. 45 inside (Top lock) - keep op in 45 degrees and elbows in . look for a high cross grip, cross cuff grip. Start to walk up th eops back and secure top lock. 

5.if opp stands up, keep cross collar grip and cross cuff grip and break posture. as they are bent you can walk legs DOWN opps back and secure a top lock. 

6. Knocking Op to ASS. if op stands up use scoop grip to get them on they ass. keep hips high and you can get them into submissions.
instructionalNotes.concept: 4 kuzushi oppertunities and 6 vulnerabilites.
instructionalNotes.warning: try to keep opps posture from standing

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Elbow Escape from Mount — Variation 5
instructionalNotes.goal: Use the rising shrimp to keep frames intact and create enough space to thread a leg through to guard.
instructionalNotes.concept: Make the KNEE the focus — everything is about creating access to the knee when opponent is tight.
instructionalNotes.details:
Primary frame = arm across hip.
Secondary frame = hand covering primary arm, elbow on opponent's knee.
Keep elbow close to opponent's hip throughout the shrimp — this preserves both frames.

Steps:
1. Establish primary frame (arm across hip) + secondary frame (elbow on knee).
2. Execute rising shrimp, keeping elbow pinned to opponent's hip.
3. Roll onto shoulder. Straighten same-side leg and thread it between opponent's legs.
4. Recover guard.
instructionalNotes.warning: Never let elbow drift toward your belly button — this collapses the frame entirely.

steps.text: Establish primary frame (arm across hip) + secondary frame (elbow on knee).
Execute rising shrimp, keeping elbow pinned to opponent's hip.
Roll onto shoulder. Straighten same-side leg and thread it between opponent's legs.
Recover guard.

stepsList.text: Establish primary frame (arm across hip) + secondary frame (elbow on knee).
Execute rising shrimp, keeping elbow pinned to opponent's hip.
Roll onto shoulder. Straighten same-side leg and thread it between opponent's legs.
Recover guard.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Elbow Escape from Mount — Variation 6
instructionalNotes.goal: Prevent underhook attacks while creating space to shimmy out to guard.
instructionalNotes.concept: Keep the armpit closed at all times — an open armpit is an invitation for armbars and kimuras from mount.
instructionalNotes.details:
After establishing both frames, get to your side and place the bottom hand (button hand) on opponent's knee.
Armpit must stay closed throughout the entire escape.

Steps:
1. Establish primary and secondary frames.
2. Get to your side. Place button hand on opponent's knee.
3. Keep armpit closed off tightly.
4. Shimmy bottom leg out to recover guard.
instructionalNotes.warning: Open armpit = immediate armbar or kimura lock vulnerability from mount.

steps.text: Establish primary and secondary frames.
Get to your side. Place button hand on opponent's knee.
Keep armpit closed off tightly.
Shimmy bottom leg out to recover guard.

stepsList.text: Establish primary and secondary frames.
Get to your side. Place button hand on opponent's knee.
Keep armpit closed off tightly.
Shimmy bottom leg out to recover guard.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Elbow Escape from Mount — Variation 7
instructionalNotes.goal: Use the cross face pressure against itself by shrimping into it, shifting opponent off and freeing the knee.
instructionalNotes.concept: Shrimp INTO the cross face direction — this tips opponent over the other side and relieves the pressure.
instructionalNotes.details:
Bump opponent first to create enough space to insert frames.
Rising shrimp toward the cross face side shifts opponent to the opposite side.
Once cross face clears, knee becomes accessible — proceed as in Variation 5.

Steps:
1. Bump opponent to create space and insert frames.
2. Rising shrimp in the direction of the cross face.
3. Opponent shifts to opposite side — cross face relieved.
4. Access the knee and shimmy out as in Variation 5.
instructionalNotes.warning: Do not shrimp away from the cross face — shrimp INTO it.

steps.text: Bump opponent to create space and insert frames.
Rising shrimp in the direction of the cross face.
Opponent shifts to opposite side — cross face relieved.
Access the knee and shimmy out as in Variation 5.

stepsList.text: Bump opponent to create space and insert frames.
Rising shrimp in the direction of the cross face.
Opponent shifts to opposite side — cross face relieved.
Access the knee and shimmy out as in Variation 5.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Ankle Trap Method 1
instructionalNotes.goal: Intercept the mount transition by trapping the foot as it becomes exposed during the pass.
instructionalNotes.concept: The foot becomes vulnerable as it falls into your thigh during the transition — this is your window.
instructionalNotes.details:
As opponent transitions, open legs in the direction of their knee sliding past your belly.
The foot becomes exposed to an ankle trap as it falls into your thigh.

Steps:
1. Feel the transition — open legs as opponent's knee slides past your belly.
2. Trap the ankle as their foot falls into your thigh.
3. Shrimp into opponent, end up in half guard.
4. Elbow escape into full guard.
instructionalNotes.warning: Timing is everything — you must open your legs as it is happening, not after.

steps.text: Feel the transition — open legs as opponent's knee slides past your belly.
Trap the ankle as their foot falls into your thigh.
Shrimp into opponent, end up in half guard.
Elbow escape into full guard.

stepsList.text: Feel the transition — open legs as opponent's knee slides past your belly.
Trap the ankle as their foot falls into your thigh.
Shrimp into opponent, end up in half guard.
Elbow escape into full guard.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Ankle Trap Method 2 — Straight to Knee
instructionalNotes.goal: Target the trailing knee directly as it becomes light and vulnerable during the transition.
instructionalNotes.concept: As opponent slides into mount, their trailing knee loses weight — go STRAIGHT TO THE KNEE immediately.
instructionalNotes.details:
As opponent slides into mount the trailing knee is lighter and vulnerable.
Do not wait — go directly to the knee before they complete the transition.

Steps:
1. Recognize the side-to-mount transition beginning.
2. Target the trailing knee immediately as it becomes light.
3. Slide the trailing knee into your guard.
4. Recover guard.
instructionalNotes.warning: Hesitation loses the window — the knee only stays light for a moment.

steps.text: Recognize the side-to-mount transition beginning.
Target the trailing knee immediately as it becomes light.
Slide the trailing knee into your guard.
Recover guard.

stepsList.text: Recognize the side-to-mount transition beginning.
Target the trailing knee immediately as it becomes light.
Slide the trailing knee into your guard.
Recover guard.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Elbow Escape from Side Pin — General Framework
instructionalNotes.goal: Get your limbs inside the wedges your opponent creates and recover guard.
instructionalNotes.concept: Ask yourself how you are being held down — the specific hold determines the correct response.
instructionalNotes.details:
Multiple hold types each require a specific response.
Make a swim motion to get near-side elbow inside to form a frame — turn away to create it.
Do not place your frame too far out. Get inside position first, then get knee inside.
Bridge your cross arm into opponent to get your KNEE inside.

Steps:
1. Identify exactly how you are being held down.
2. Swim near-side elbow inside to form frame — turn away from opponent.
3. Do not overextend the frame — get inside position first.
4. Bridge cross arm into opponent to create space.
5. Get knee inside, then get the rest of your limbs in.
instructionalNotes.warning: Do not place your frame too far out — it will be ineffective and overextended.

steps.text: Identify exactly how you are being held down.
Swim near-side elbow inside to form frame — turn away from opponent.
Do not overextend the frame — get inside position first.
Bridge cross arm into opponent to create space.
Get knee inside, then get the rest of your limbs in.

stepsList.text: Identify exactly how you are being held down.
Swim near-side elbow inside to form frame — turn away from opponent.
Do not overextend the frame — get inside position first.
Bridge cross arm into opponent to create space.
Get knee inside, then get the rest of your limbs in.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Elbow Escape from Side Pin 2 — Head Control
instructionalNotes.goal: Move opponent's head to the same side as their hips to make knee wedge entry much easier.
instructionalNotes.concept: When opponent's head is on the same side as their hips, knee entry becomes significantly easier.
instructionalNotes.details:
Move opponent's head across using a bicep cross face or forearm cross face.
This repositions their weight and opens the path for knee entry.

Steps:
1. Apply bicep cross face or forearm cross face.
2. Drive opponent's head to the same side as their hips.
3. Wedge knee in once the path is open.
4. Get remaining limbs inside and recover guard.
instructionalNotes.warning: Cannot always execute this, but it makes a significant difference when available.

steps.text: Apply bicep cross face or forearm cross face.
Drive opponent's head to the same side as their hips.
Wedge knee in once the path is open.
Get remaining limbs inside and recover guard.

stepsList.text: Apply bicep cross face or forearm cross face.
Drive opponent's head to the same side as their hips.
Wedge knee in once the path is open.
Get remaining limbs inside and recover guard.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Elbow Escape from Side Pin 3 — Hip Line Entry
instructionalNotes.goal: Enter from the hip line, shrimp out, and introduce the knee.
instructionalNotes.concept: Enter along the hip line — this is the path of least resistance to get your knee inside.
instructionalNotes.details:
Approach the escape by entering from the hip line rather than fighting through the upper body.

Steps:
1. Enter your framing from the hip line.
2. Shrimp out to create space.
3. Introduce knee and recover guard.
instructionalNotes.warning: Do not fight through the upper body — go to the hip line.

steps.text: Enter your framing from the hip line.
Shrimp out to create space.
Introduce knee and recover guard.

stepsList.text: Enter your framing from the hip line.
Shrimp out to create space.
Introduce knee and recover guard.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Elbow Escape from Side Pin 4 — Strong Cross Face
instructionalNotes.goal: Walk away to create space, forcing opponent to relieve pressure, then insert knee.
instructionalNotes.concept: Walk AWAY from opponent in a strong cross face — this forces them to chase you and relieve pressure.
instructionalNotes.details:
The cross face is always a challenge but it can be managed.
Walking away creates space and forces opponent to address the gap.
Insert knee when space opens up.
Part 2: push into opponent, tip them to get a reaction, then shoot knee in.

Steps:
1. Establish frames.
2. Walk away from opponent — force them to relieve cross face pressure.
3. Insert knee when space opens.
4. If stuck: push into opponent to generate a reaction.
5. Tip opponent and shoot knee in to recover guard.
instructionalNotes.warning: Do not fight the cross face head-on — walking away is counter-intuitive but effective.

steps.text: Establish frames.
Walk away from opponent — force them to relieve cross face pressure.
Insert knee when space opens.
If stuck: push into opponent to generate a reaction.
Tip opponent and shoot knee in to recover guard.

stepsList.text: Establish frames.
Walk away from opponent — force them to relieve cross face pressure.
Insert knee when space opens.
If stuck: push into opponent to generate a reaction.
Tip opponent and shoot knee in to recover guard.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Elbow Escape from Side Pin 7 — Hand on Same Side Hip
instructionalNotes.goal: Shrimp and pummel the bicep to create space, bring knee inside the bicep.
instructionalNotes.concept: Shrimp against the bicep specifically — this creates the pocket to bring your knee through.
instructionalNotes.details:
Shrimp and pummel your bicep in against opponent.
Shrimp against their bicep to open the gap.
Knee enters inside the bicep.

Steps:
1. Shrimp and pummel your bicep in against opponent.
2. Shrimp against opponent's bicep to create space.
3. Bring knee inside the bicep.
4. Recover guard.
instructionalNotes.warning: Knee must go inside the bicep — not above or outside it.

steps.text: Shrimp and pummel your bicep in against opponent.
Shrimp against opponent's bicep to create space.
Bring knee inside the bicep.
Recover guard.

stepsList.text: Shrimp and pummel your bicep in against opponent.
Shrimp against opponent's bicep to create space.
Bring knee inside the bicep.
Recover guard.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Knee Escapes from Side Pin 1 + 2 — Underhook to One Knee
instructionalNotes.goal: Escape the side pin with an underhook and get to one knee.
instructionalNotes.concept: When facing strong pressure, bridge HARD into opponent first before attempting the underhook.
instructionalNotes.details:
Bridging hard into opponent relieves pressure enough to create movement.
Get your face to opponent's chest so you can move better.
Use one hand to manage.

Steps:
1. Bridge hard into opponent to relieve pressure.
2. Get face to opponent's chest.
3. Fight for the underhook.
4. Get to one knee and recover.
instructionalNotes.warning: Do not try to underhook before bridging — bridge first to create the space.

steps.text: Bridge hard into opponent to relieve pressure.
Get face to opponent's chest.
Fight for the underhook.
Get to one knee and recover.

stepsList.text: Bridge hard into opponent to relieve pressure.
Get face to opponent's chest.
Fight for the underhook.
Get to one knee and recover.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Knee Escape from Side Pin 3 — Underhook vs Resisting Opponent
instructionalNotes.goal: Use frames and forehead pressure to sink the underhook, then get to single leg.
instructionalNotes.concept: Move your forehead to opponent's chest first — this creates the body angle needed to sink the underhook.
instructionalNotes.details:
Frame on the near shoulder.
Move forehead to opponent's chest.
Sink underhook from that position.
If shut out: use bicep cross face to bridge, get near-side hand between face and shoulder.
Key JD detail: STEP on the calf muscle as you look for the underhook, then transition to single leg takedown.
Heavy pressure from opponent's locked hands usually opens the hip for knee entry.

Steps:
1. Frame on near shoulder.
2. Move forehead to opponent's chest.
3. Sink underhook.
4. If shut out: bicep cross face to bridge, get near-side hand between face and shoulder.
5. Use position to bridge to single leg — step on calf muscle.
6. Heavy pressure from locked hands opens the hip — use knee entry then single leg takedown.
instructionalNotes.warning: If shut out, do not abandon — use the bicep cross face bridge to reset.

steps.text: Frame on near shoulder.
Move forehead to opponent's chest.
Sink underhook.
If shut out: bicep cross face to bridge, get near-side hand between face and shoulder.
Use position to bridge to single leg — step on calf muscle.
Heavy pressure from locked hands opens the hip — use knee entry then single leg takedown.

stepsList.text: Frame on near shoulder.
Move forehead to opponent's chest.
Sink underhook.
If shut out: bicep cross face to bridge, get near-side hand between face and shoulder.
Use position to bridge to single leg — step on calf muscle.
Heavy pressure from locked hands opens the hip — use knee entry then single leg takedown.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Side Pin Sit Thru Escape 1 — Anticipating the Move
instructionalNotes.goal: Anticipate the sit thru and take your elbow to the mat before opponent can exploit it.
instructionalNotes.concept: TAKE your elbow to the mat proactively — do not let opponent take it.
instructionalNotes.details:
Anticipate that opponent is going to move into the sit thru.
Elbow goes to the mat immediately.
Bottom arm moves across opponent's chest, elbow on their hip.
Shrimp out with the frames you have.

Steps:
1. Anticipate the sit thru movement.
2. Take your elbow to the mat immediately — do not let them take it.
3. Move bottom arm across opponent's chest, elbow on their hip.
4. Shrimp out using your frames.
5. Regain guard.
instructionalNotes.warning: Elbow to the mat is the priority — if opponent gets your elbow, you are in a bad spot.

steps.text: Anticipate the sit thru movement.
Take your elbow to the mat immediately — do not let them take it.
Move bottom arm across opponent's chest, elbow on their hip.
Shrimp out using your frames.
Regain guard.

stepsList.text: Anticipate the sit thru movement.
Take your elbow to the mat immediately — do not let them take it.
Move bottom arm across opponent's chest, elbow on their hip.
Shrimp out using your frames.
Regain guard.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Side Pin Sit Thru Escape 2 — Failed to Anticipate
instructionalNotes.goal: Post against opponent's bicep and jaw to yank the trapped arm free, then recover guard.
instructionalNotes.concept: Post on the bicep and jaw to create the lever that frees your trapped arm — be careful of back exposure.
instructionalNotes.details:
Opponent gets your arm — you are in a bad spot.
Post against opponent's bicep and jaw to yank trapped arm free.
Recover guard as in Escape 1.
If knee is hard to get in, use a REVERSE SHRIMP to create space.

Steps:
1. Post against opponent's bicep and jaw.
2. Yank trapped arm free — watch for back exposure.
3. Recover guard as in Escape 1.
4. If knee hard to insert: use reverse shrimp to create space.
instructionalNotes.warning: Watch for back exposure when yanking the arm free.

steps.text: Post against opponent's bicep and jaw.
Yank trapped arm free — watch for back exposure.
Recover guard as in Escape 1.
If knee hard to insert: use reverse shrimp to create space.

stepsList.text: Post against opponent's bicep and jaw.
Yank trapped arm free — watch for back exposure.
Recover guard as in Escape 1.
If knee hard to insert: use reverse shrimp to create space.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Pin Escapes & Turtle Escapes
instructionalNotes.name: Side Pin Sit Thru Escape 2 — Reverse Sit Thru
instructionalNotes.goal: Fight for elbow position as opponent transitions and use shrimp to bring knee in.
instructionalNotes.concept: Keep elbow in and tucked throughout — elbow cannot be on the outside.
instructionalNotes.details:
As opponent transfers to the reverse sit thru, fight for elbow position immediately.
Keep elbow tucked inside — never let it go outside.
Shrimp into opponent to force a reaction and bring knee in.
When opponent attempts mount transition: hear the foot slap, BRIDGE back — mechanically weak pass.
Against heavy opponents: sneak into bottom half guard as they transition.

Steps:
1. Fight for elbow position as reverse sit thru begins.
2. Keep elbow tucked inside — cannot be outside.
3. Shrimp into opponent to force a reaction.
4. Bring knee in and pull opponent back to guard.
5. If foot slap heard: bridge back in the direction you came from.
instructionalNotes.warning: Elbow must stay inside and tucked — outside elbow is immediately exploitable.

steps.text: Fight for elbow position as reverse sit thru begins.
Keep elbow tucked inside — cannot be outside.
Shrimp into opponent to force a reaction.
Bring knee in and pull opponent back to guard.
If foot slap heard: bridge back in the direction you came from.

stepsList.text: Fight for elbow position as reverse sit thru begins.
Keep elbow tucked inside — cannot be outside.
Shrimp into opponent to force a reaction.
Bring knee in and pull opponent back to guard.
If foot slap heard: bridge back in the direction you came from.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: Understanding A Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.concept:
Close guard keep oppopent in place so they can move in or out.
Use legs to break balence forward. Get rid of the frames. Use a knee pull, forward pull.
instructionalNotes.details:
keep hands of chest for knee pulls. 
when oppoent is looking to step up, knee pull on em to break balence.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: Closed Guard Overview
instructionalNotes.concept:
Top Hip Principle - The athletes whos hips are above another will always win top subs.
Hold Close guard long enough to work 
Effective response to all 4 scenarios - Opp on 2 knees, opp goes to stand, opp stood but bent, and opp stands tall.
Break opp stance and balance 
Forward kuzushi - opp on 2 knees, opp bent over op standing
Sidways kuzushi - opp on 2 knees
Backwards kazushi- opp stands tall

6 vulnerable situations from Closed :
 Elbow across center line -> Side Scissor Position 
Top lock - > 45 inside , head below hips
Trap triangle-> inside wrist position.
Hand on floor - > Clamp position 
Opp goes to stand in close guard  -> over head sweep ( 2nd step )
Hips on mat
instructionalNotes.details:
Devastating attack position.
Crucial skill - retrun standing opp back to mat, double ankle sweep, scoop hand stand sweep, omoplata sweep

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: What Is The Central Message Of This Video?
instructionalNotes.goal:
close guard is an offensive position, not a defensive position.  
top hip position , results in a mounted postion from a sweep
have access to back takes
top hip position also has access to lots of submission ( its like being in mount)
instructionalNotes.concept:
Opp has limited options from being in your closed guard,
who ever has top position is in a better position

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: The First Three Abilities We Need From Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.goal: break opp posture and balance using KUZUSHI it the goal. need to drill this to get a sense of it. super important.
instructionalNotes.concept:
key abitlites to have a good closed guard

1. Hold opp in closed guard long enough to have something to work with ..

Scenarios we willl work with 

1. opp is on 2 knees (preferred)
2. opp goes to stand on 2 feet (can catch opp in between the step ups, next preferred)
3. opp stood up but bent over
4. opp stood up and has good posture

3. skill to break the posture and balance 

a. kuzushi in 3 directions 

forwards kuzushi - break opp forward  with a knee pull is the most used  method comes as a result with LEGS and HIPS
even if opp stands up on 2 feet PULL knees in, it makes it harder for your opp.
best used if opps head is over hips

 back wards kuzushi-  which is best used when opp head is over hips ( standing) can take them back to sit down.

***sidways kuzushi  - the most used form of kuzushi, taking the opp to the side droping them to a shoplder and hip .

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: The Six Vulnerabilities Inside A Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.goal: If you can put your opp in 1 of these 6 vulnerabilities (situations) you will be able to be effective against a resisting opp.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: The Six Vulnerabilities - Part 1
instructionalNotes.goal: Take opponents elbow past the center line
instructionalNotes.concept:
Bringing the elbow across to the center line you can go into SIDE SCISSOR and take back or sweep. 

Work on both arms.

If one arm is sunken down holding grip, go for the other arm. don't fixate on one.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: The Six Vulnerabilities - Part 2
instructionalNotes.goal: Establish top lock for arm lock attacks.
instructionalNotes.concept: lot of control of opp head and shoulders, can keep your opp broken forward. shorter distance to cover from a top lock

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: The Six Vulnerabilities - Part 3
instructionalNotes.goal: Establish Trap Triangle.
instructionalNotes.concept: Essentially break opps posture one hugging head to chest and the other and holding the WRIST from the side you will enter from.  control the wrist and you will be able to clear the arm for a trap triangle.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: The Six Vulnerabilities - Part 4
instructionalNotes.goal: Bring opps hands on the floor one or bothand move into the clamp
instructionalNotes.concept:
If you can put hands on the floor the opp has a harder time to base and link arms togehter

use basically break opp down with a knee pull but MAINTAIN inside are position ( t-rex arms) to take up space. swim out a bit then get to the clamp. Isolate the head and a shoulder.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: The Six Vulnerabilities - Part 5
instructionalNotes.goal: Kuzushi while opp is standing up. Knee pull on the 2nd step
instructionalNotes.concept:
JD goes into detail explaining that as your opp goes to stand there is a big displacement o weight and head position happening. the first step up is sturdy its thw SECOND where the opp has to lean the other way to get the leg up whwere we can take advange

as opp takes first step up start to drive heels to opp but it creates more pressure for opp to stand, as the second leg starts to move KNEE PULL opp into you.

instructionalNotes.seriesName: Go Further Faster — Closed Guard
instructionalNotes.name: The Six Vulnerabilities - Part 6
instructionalNotes.goal: Get ops HIPS and ASS on the ground.
instructionalNotes.concept: Scoop grip when opp stands up or picks one leg up ALWAYS.
